title: Funeral Homes and Funeral Services

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $5,800.0M / sectoral output (T30) $17,793.8M, year 2022
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

occupations.title: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $162,073 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 812210); class <5: 4,937 firms, avg revenue/firm $548,492 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $85,839 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,060 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,134,884 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,609 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,786 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,855,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $290,334 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 893 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,331,400 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $677,864 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,374,525 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,875,613 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $423,789,444 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $66,323,048 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 40
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Licensed, relational, physical work (arrangement conferences, removals, service direction) is not automatable; the AI-touchable sliver is paperwork wrapped inside the role: death certificates, permits, insurance assignments, obituary drafting. AI obituary/case-doc tools (Tribute, Passare) already do this. ~15% of the role's hours are that paperwork.
Often licensee-owners; scheduling, compliance filings, pre-need administration and reporting automate; community presence, family relationships and staff leadership do not.
Physical service work: visitation staffing, casket movement, processions. Not automatable by current AI.
Licensed physical preparation and cremation operations. Not automatable.
Physical grounds work; largely cemetery contamination from the 4-digit 8122 proxy (812220), not funeral-home staff. Zero regardless.

Case administration, filings, billing, aftercare correspondence, phone handling - the clean AI surface. Half the work is substantially doable by current AI; in-person reception and grieving-family phone contact remains human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services (41-3091, pre-need sales)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Pre-need selling is relationship/trust work; the heavy contract paperwork and follow-up correspondence automates.
Removal/transport driving and physical support work is not automatable; small scheduling/dispatch/admin sliver is. Remainder = 1 - sum of listed wage shares (0.8045).

pi_dist.rationale: The firm owns the client directly: consumer-paid, at-need purchase with strong local price opacity, grief-constrained shopping, and no upstream gatekeeper, platform or procurement layer. Pricing is itemized fixed/per-unit under the FTC Funeral Rule GPL, not cost-plus or hourly, so AI admin savings are retained margin by default - no billing mechanism hands them to clients. Docked from 1.0 because the FTC Funeral Rule review (potential mandatory online price posting) and online direct-cremation price transparency are live threats to the opacity that supports retention.

pi_moat.rationale: Real barriers: state funeral director/embalmer licensing everywhere, physical premises, and multi-generational family-name community trust. Generic AI cannot bypass the firm - disposition is licensed and physical. But the bypass that matters is non-AI: online direct-cremation brands (Tulip, Solace, Aftercare-style entrants) that never use the acquired firm's premises, home funerals, and celebration-of-life venues unbundling the service; cremation share at 63.4% (2025) heading to 82.3% (2045) structurally erodes what the moat protects. Also acquisition-specific risk: case-volume attrition when the selling director exits even if the family name is retained.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct ~10% materially using AI today (18% 'using or planning within a year', so material current use lower). Historical analog: this is a documented slow-adopter industry - websites took roughly 12-15 years from mid-1990s introduction to majority penetration (>85% only by 2022), livestreaming needed a pandemic shock to spike. But AI obituary/admin tools are being pushed free-of-charge through incumbent vendor platforms already installed in most homes (Tribute Technology, Passare, Tukios), which compresses the distribution lag that slowed websites. From ~10% today, reaching 50% material use in the automatable functions ~ 6 years (roughly half the website curve).

current_adoption_pct.source: Gitnux - AI In The Funeral Industry Statistics (aggregated industry survey data); corroborated by Washington Post reporting on AI obituary tools
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 18% of funeral directors are currently using AI or plan to implement it within the next year

historical_analogs.value: Funeral home websites: introduced mid-1990s, majority penetration only in the late 2000s/2010s (~12-15 years to ~50%), >85% by 2022. Livestreaming: available since ~2010, minority adoption until COVID forced a 300% surge in 2020. CRM adoption still only ~35%. Consistent pattern: 10-15 year adoption curves for practice technology in this industry.
historical_analogs.source: WifiTalents funeral industry statistics compilation; University of Melbourne DeathTech research on slow deathcare tech adoption
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: over 85% having an active online presence as of 2022; approximately 35% of funeral homes utilize CRM systems; live streaming services increased by 300% during the pandemic

owners_60plus_pct.source: Obitley industry analysis citing NFDA owner-demographic estimates; corroborated by Connecting Directors coverage of succession-planning study
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: NFDA estimates 38% of independent owner-operators are 60+, and only 24% have a documented succession plan

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: An estimated 28% of independent funeral home owners are over 65 with no identified successor; only 24% of owner-operators have a documented succession plan; nearly half plan to retire within five years while less than a quarter have a succession plan (NFDA-cited figures via Obitley/Connecting Directors, accessed 2026-07-20).

active_consolidators.source: Funeral Director Daily M&A coverage; KFF Health News; The Shoestring consolidation reporting. Platforms with add-ons in last 24 months: SCI, Park Lawn (Homesteaders/Birch Hill, private since Aug 2024), Carriage Services, Foundation Partners Group (recapitalized 2025), Rollings Funeral Service, Pinnacle Funeral Service, Legacy Funeral Group, Milestone/Rosewood Private Investments
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: SCI spent more than $181M on 26 funeral service properties and six cemeteries in 2024; 'Rollings and Pinnacle are privately held buyers paying strong multiples'; 'In 2024 Milestone was acquired by Rosewood Private Investments'

buy_mult.source: Creedy & Co. funeral home valuation series (specialist deathcare appraiser), corroborated by CT Acquisitions broker guidance (4-6x for single-location homes)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: the range of purchase multiples heard most frequently is 4 to 6.5 times EBITDA, with 5 times EBITDA representing the neutral point or average multiple for a decent but unremarkable firm

exit_mult.source: GuruFocus SCI EV/EBITDA (public platform comp); Park Lawn C$1.2B take-private at 62.1% premium (Aug 2024) consistent with low-double-digit adjusted EBITDA multiple
exit_mult.figure_quoted: SERVICE INTERNATIONAL EV-to-EBITDA: 11.89 - Near Median (10-year median 12.19); Park Lawn acquired for C$26.50/share, ~C$1.2 billion including net debt

pricing_structure.value: ~100% itemized fixed/per-unit consumer-paid pricing under the FTC Funeral Rule General Price List (services fee, casket, embalming, facilities, transport itemized per case). No hourly, retainer or cost-plus billing. Median funeral with viewing and burial $8,300 (2023); median funeral with cremation $6,280; online direct cremation sold at roughly $1,000-2,500, a fraction of burial revenue per case. Pre-need contracts (trust- or insurance-funded) lock future volume at locked-in prices.
pricing_structure.source: NFDA 2025 Cremation & Burial Report release and NFDA GPL survey medians
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: projected 2025 cremation rate is 63.4%... by 2045, cremation is expected to reach 82.3%; median cost of a funeral with viewing and burial was $8,300; median cost of a funeral with cremation was $6,280

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw 0.0471885 = 4.71885pp of revenue as potential EBITDA uplift (on ~20-25% normalized EBITDA margins, a meaningful ~20% relative uplift). A = 1.667 < 5, so the low-uplift/high-A inconsistency test does not trigger; uplift is above 3pp in any case. Consistent.

terminal_value.justification: AI cuts the operator's costs (the ~11% office/admin wage share plus paperwork slivers inside licensed roles); it does not replace the product - mature AI does not embalm, transport, conduct services or sit with grieving families. But demand economics shrink structurally without AI: case volume grows demographically while revenue per case falls as cremation share climbs from 63.4% (2025) toward 82.3% (2045) and online direct-cremation entrants (selling at ~$1,000-2,500 vs $8,300 median burial) disintermediate the premises entirely. 'Deaths are increasing' does NOT mean revenue is safe: mix shift is a secular revenue-per-case decline independent of AI, and the ~4.7pp AI cost uplift is roughly one cremation-mix decade's worth of revenue erosion, not a transformation. Demand for licensed disposition persists indefinitely, so not MELTING; but the traditional full-service funeral home's unit economics are being squeezed, so PRESSURED, not DURABLE.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was computed solely from the provided labor_share and role_mix with within-role automatable fractions justified by task content (paperwork vs licensed/physical/relational work). A was derived from a current-adoption survey figure and the industry's own website/livestreaming/CRM adoption history. No deal activity, consolidation trend, or PE interest was used for V or A; consolidator counts and multiples inform only B (CFD) and M.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is ~100% itemized fixed/per-unit consumer pricing under the FTC Funeral Rule GPL - no hourly, cost-plus or audited billing exists to mechanically pass AI savings to clients, so efficiency gains default to operator margin. This supports the high pi_dist (0.85). The real deflation channels are not billing-model but (a) price transparency: online direct-cremation advertising and the FTC's potential mandatory online GPL posting compress local price opacity (reflected in pi_dist dock from 1.0), and (b) mix: cremation share growth cuts revenue per case regardless of billing model (reflected in pi_moat 0.65 and the PRESSURED terminal class).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 1.0 (t50=10) to 2.5 (t50=4); S moves roughly 3.05 to 3.66 holding others fixed - the single largest swing factor.
Feeds t50 only; at 18% material use today t50 could be ~5 (A=2.0, S~3.5); at 5% t50 nearer 8 (A=1.25, S~3.2). LOW-quality aggregator source.
M ranges 3.2 to 5.6; S moves roughly 3.11 to 3.48. Platform exits for private $10M+ EBITDA groups are not publicly disclosed; SCI public multiple is the proxy.
V ranges 1.30 to 2.61; S moves roughly 3.13 to 3.61.

reviewer_note: Formula results reported as computed; no overrides. Points of note: (1) n_band=40 is used as given per rule 6 but is almost certainly a floor - the derivation's generic 15.65% margin is below the 20-25%+ normalized EBITDA margins healthy funeral homes run, and at realistic margins part of the 893-firm 20-99 employee class (avg revenue $4.3M) would clear $1M EBITDA; TD (0.40) and hence B are understated if so (n_band=900 would give TD~0.74, B~6.7, S~3.55). (2) role_mix is 4-digit 8122 including 812220 Cemeteries and Crematories - the 7.7% groundskeeper share is largely cemetery contamination; I set it to zero automatable so it dilutes ai_replaceable_share slightly downward, which is conservative for V. (3) buy_mult 5.0x is quoted by specialist appraisers on normalized EBITDA after owner comp; small-home listings quoted on SDE run higher nominal prices - I used the EBITDA-basis figure per rule 3. Family owner comp, owner real estate at non-market rent, and pre-need trust income all require normalization in any real diligence. (4) Pre-need books cut both ways in acquisitions: locked volume but below-inflation locked pricing; treated as neutral in scoring. (5) current_adoption_pct and historical_analogs rest on LOW-quality aggregator statistics; primary NFDA member-survey AI data was not accessible without membership. (6) Cross-check 2: the dominant economic force here is cremation-mix revenue deflation, not AI - the roll-up case is a B/M story (fragmented aging-owner base, wide buy/exit multiple arbitrage) with a modest V.
